description: This test case evaluates the generator model's accuracy and technical depth, assuming a perfect retrieval process.
question: Explain how the in-plane compositional variation of materials in the pellicle membrane, as described in US20230333462A1, contributes to enhanced performance in EUV lithography systems. Discuss the manufacturing steps that enable this variation and the functional advantages it provides over uniform pellicle membranes.

gold_context_sources.evidence: [0055] The grid is embedded within the pellicle membrane and forms parts of the pellicle membrane itself, as opposed to existing pellicle membranes which do not have an integral grid portion, even though they may have a supportive grid. In contrast, in existing pellicle membranes, there would be no difference in the materials which are intersected by the line A-A as it is moved across the surface of the pellicle membrane as existing pellicles have a uniform surface. The pellicle membrane of the present direction may preferably vary in the x and y direction (where the z-direction corresponds to the thickness). [0056] Further processing steps as are known in the art, such as back-side etching to etch away a portion of the substrate to leave a border to support the pellicle membrane, may be conducted in order to arrive at a final pellicle assembly.

[0056] FIGS. 3a to 3g depict a method for manufacturing a pellicle membrane according to the present invention. In a first step, a substrate 18 is provided and a sacrificial layer 19 is provided on the substrate 18. The substrate 18 preferably comprises silicon. In the next step, a first material layer 20 is provided on the sacrificial layer 19. Following this, a photoresist layer 21 is provided on the surface of the first material layer 20. The photoresist layer 21 is then patterned to define the layout of the grid of the final pellicle membrane. Once the photoresist layer 21 has been patterned, an etching step takes place which etches the underlying first material layer 20 to form a patterned surface. In an embodiment, the next step in the method is the deposition of a layer of a second material 22 on the patterned surface. As shown in FIG. 3f, the second material 22 is deposited within the channels formed by the earlier etching step as well as on top of the remaining areas including the photoresist layer 21. The remaining photoresist layer 21 and the overlying second material layer 22 may be lifted off to provide a pellicle membrane comprising the first material 20 and the second material 22.

[0057] Subsequently, the top surface of the pellicle membrane may be planarized and/or polished to provide a planar surface. Polishing may be effected by ion polishing. In another embodiment, remaining portions of the photoresist layer 21 may be removed before the second material layer 22 is deposited. As such, the second material layer 22 may be provided on top of the first material layer 20 and would also be provided within the channels created by the patterning step. In order to provide a pellicle membrane with a planar surface, the excess second material 22 may be removed by planarizing. Planarizing may be effected by chemical-mechanical planarization, polishing, and/or etching.

ground_truth_answer:
The pellicle membrane described in US20230333462A1 features in-plane compositional variation, specifically comprising at least two different materials arranged such that one forms a grid structure embedded within the pellicle membrane, and the other forms distinct regions. This design contrasts with traditional pellicle membranes, which have uniform composition and no integral grid. The in-plane variation allows for functional advantages such as enhanced EUV transmission in regions composed of high-transmissivity material and improved emissivity or filtering of undesired wavelengths by the grid material. The grid's higher emissivity aids in thermal management, reducing heat accumulation during EUV exposure, thereby improving imaging performance and pellicle durability. Moreover, the grid can filter out undesired electromagnetic wavelengths, protecting the lithographic system and enhancing image fidelity.

Manufacturing this pellicle membrane involves several precise steps: starting with a silicon substrate coated with a sacrificial layer, a first material layer is deposited. A photoresist layer is then applied and patterned to define the grid layout. Subsequent etching forms channels in the first material layer according to the pattern. A second material layer is deposited, filling the etched channels and covering the photoresist. The photoresist and overlying second material are then lifted off, leaving the composite membrane with the embedded grid. The surface is then planarized and polished, often by ion polishing or chemical-mechanical planarization, to achieve a flat and uniform surface. Additional processing, such as back-side etching of the substrate, may be done to finalize the pellicle assembly.

This in-plane compositional variation enables tailored optical and thermal properties that surpass those of uniform membranes, providing enhanced EUV transmittance where needed and improved thermal emission and filtering by the grid. The integral grid within the pellicle membrane structure also provides mechanical stability and can reduce contamination risks. Overall, the design and manufacturing method afford a pellicle membrane that maximizes EUV lithography system performance, longevity, and reliability compared to conventional uniform pellicles.
